title: From Gromov–Witten Theory to the Closing Lemma
speaker: Shira Tanny
abstract: An old question of Poincaré concerns creating periodic orbits via perturbations of a flow/diffeomorphism. While pseudoholomorphic methods have successfully addressed this question in dimensions 2-3, the higher-dimensional case remains less understood. I will describe a connection between this question and Gromov–Witten invariants, which goes through a new class of invariants of symplectic cobordisms. This is a joint work with Julian Chaidez.

proof_stages.label: Closing lemma and strong closing property
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates the closing problem for Hamiltonian diffeomorphisms and Reeb flows, recalls known C^1 and low-dimensional C^[0minfinity results, and introduces the stronger requirement that every nonnegative local deformation create a periodic orbit at some parameter.

proof_stages.label: Shift from dynamics to change-measuring cobordism invariants
proof_stages.summary: Motivated by limitations of existing contact/Floer homological proofs and by the Fisch-Hofer speculation relating closing to rich Gromov-Witten theory, the speaker proposes constructing holomorphic-curve invariants that measure the change between two systems, geometrically encoded by a symplectic cobordism.

proof_stages.label: Conformal stable Hamiltonian framework
proof_stages.summary: Contact manifolds and Hamiltonian mapping tori are unified as conformal stable Hamiltonian manifolds, whose conformality gives a canonical symplectization and allows subgraph cobordisms to encode perturbations.

proof_stages.label: Definition of SFT spectral gaps
proof_stages.summary: For fixed topological data, the SFT spectral gap of a completed cobordism is defined as a supremum over almost complex structures and point constraints of the minimum area of a punctured holomorphic curve satisfying genus, homology, point, and top-period bounds.

proof_stages.label: Structural properties of the gaps
proof_stages.summary: The analytic construction yields Hofer continuity, monotonicity/capacity lower bounds, and spectrality in a difference spectrum, with later questions clarifying monotonicity and the area-spectrum correction.

proof_stages.label: Closing criterion from small gaps
proof_stages.summary: If the infimum of the spectral gaps of a conformal stable Hamiltonian manifold is zero and the relevant two-form is rational, then a subgraph deformation cannot keep the gap both continuous/spectral and nonconstant, so absence of newly created periodic orbits gives a contradiction.

proof_stages.label: Gromov-Witten computation of small gaps
proof_stages.summary: To verify the small-gap hypothesis one seeks uniform upper bounds for all almost complex structures, obtained by embedding a thin cobordism into a closed symplectic manifold where nonzero Gromov-Witten invariants force closed curves that can be broken to produce the required cobordism curves.

examples.name: Herman-type higher-dimensional counterexamples
examples.note: The counterexamples illustrate the obstruction to a general smooth closing lemma and later clarify why rationality/spectral discreteness hypotheses are not merely cosmetic.
examples.engagement_reason: Herman-type counterexamples are invoked as higher-dimensional obstructions and later said to fail the rationality/conformality hypotheses, but the speaker does not work through the construction or derive these facts.

passes.reason: Herman-type examples are invoked to show the higher-dimensional C-infinity closing lemma can fail.

examples.name: Reeb flows on ellipsoids
examples.note: Ellipsoids are presented as the simplest higher-dimensional Reeb-flow test case for the strong closing property and as context for why a new approach is sought.
examples.engagement_reason: Reeb flows on ellipsoids are described as rotations and cited as the target of Irie’s conjecture and later proofs, but no computation or construction is carried out on a particular ellipsoid.

passes.reason: Toric ellipsoid Reeb flows are highly regular rotational model flows used as a positive test case.
standard ellipsoid Reeb flows are toric/linear highly regular positive test cases for strong closing
Ellipsoid Reeb flows are highly regular toric linear rotation flows, used here as a positive test case for strong closing.
Ellipsoid Reeb flows are linear toric rotation flows used as positive test cases for strong closing.
Ellipsoid Reeb flows form a systematic toric/integrable family used as positive test cases for strong closing, not as failures.
Reeb flows on ellipsoids form a systematic rotationally toric, highly regular test family used for a positive strong-closing result, not to refute it.
Reeb flows on ellipsoid boundaries are used as positive test cases for strong closing, and are structurally special through toric linear regularity.
Used as a positive higher-dimensional test case for strong closing; ellipsoid Reeb flows are highly regular toric rotation systems, not sporadic or degenerate.
Toric ellipsoid Reeb flows are linear rotation flows used as a positive strong-closing test case.
Used as a positive test case for strong closing, and ellipsoid Reeb dynamics are toric/linear and highly regular rather than sporadic or limiting.
A positive test case whose ellipsoid Reeb dynamics are toric linear rotations.
used as a positive model for the strong closing property; ellipsoid Reeb flows are toric linear rotation flows with high coordinate-circle symmetry.

examples.name: Zoll/free circle-action flow
examples.note: The free S^1-action case is used to reveal the key computation idea: complete the circle bundle to an S^2-bundle, use a nonzero Gromov-Witten invariant in the fiber class, and break the closed curve to obtain a curve in the thin cobordism.
examples.engagement_reason: For a Zoll/free circle-action flow, the speaker explicitly models y as an S1-bundle, completes it to an S2-bundle, embeds the thin cobordism, and uses/breaks fiber-class holomorphic curves to bound the spectral gap.

passes.reason: A Zoll/free circle-action flow is a maximally regular periodic-flow model used for computation, not refutation.
a Zoll/free circle-action flow has all trajectories organized by a free S1 action and is used as a regular computation example
A Zoll flow is a free S1-action with all orbits in the same periodic family, making it maximally regular and dynamically collapsed.

A Zoll/free circle-action flow is maximally regular in that all trajectories come from a free S1-action, and here it is used as a computable model rather than a counterexample.

Used as a computable model, not an obstruction; a Zoll/free circle-action flow is maximally regular with all orbits coming from an S^1-action.
A Zoll flow is a regular free circle action with every orbit closed and equal-period, used for a Gromov-Witten computation rather than as a refutation.

Used as a model computation case where the flow is a free S1 action with all orbits uniformly periodic.

examples.name: CP^2 versus CP^n moduli-dimension comparison
examples.note: In response to a question, projective spaces are used to illustrate why low-dimensional holomorphic-curve theories have more low-area curves available than higher-dimensional ones.
examples.engagement_reason: The CP2 versus CPn comparison is used to state the different moduli-dimension growth rates and their significance, but no formula is derived or calculation worked through.

passes.reason: CP^2 is the low-dimensional boundary case in the CP^n comparison and projective spaces are homogeneous symmetric models.
